title: Team Onboarding
text: Build one shared final-project base with three aligned modules: time series visual analysis, time series clustering, and forecasting.

title: Team Onboarding
text: Build one shared final-project base with three aligned modules: time series visual analysis, time series clustering, and forecasting.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Suggested Team Split

Member A: Time Series Visual Analysis and common data prep.
Member B: Time Series Clustering and clustering module integration.
Member C: Forecasting module and forecast evaluation.
Member D: Integration, proposal alignment, storyboard, QA, and deployment.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Branching Convention

Create branches as codex/<module-name> or feature/<module-name>.
Keep pull requests scoped to one module or one documentation layer at a time.
Merge only after quarto render and app smoke tests pass.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Local Setup
git clone <repo-url>
cd group_project
quarto preview
Rscript run_app.R 3838

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Meeting Records

First Meeting Record
Add a second meeting record after the instructor-directed pivot to the time-series direction.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Shared Files to Update Carefully

_quarto.yml for navigation and build behavior
Proposal/Proposal.qmd for the shared project narrative
prototype/ui-storyboard.qmd for the final app design
app/app.R for module registration
styles/theme.scss for global styling

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
This page is structured as an individual Take-home Exercise 2 submission and focuses on one prototype module:
Selected module: Cluster Analysis for monthly tourism market states.
The module uses monthly observations to identify interpretable tourism states:

Pandemic shock months.
Recovery transition months.
High-performance months.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
This page is structured as an individual Take-home Exercise 2 submission and focuses on one prototype module:
Selected module: Cluster Analysis for monthly tourism market states.
The module uses monthly observations to identify interpretable tourism states:

Pandemic shock months.
Recovery transition months.
High-performance months.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
1. Data Preparation Process
Data preparation follows the imported bilingual plan and detailed cleaning notes in data/references/.
Key preparation decisions:

Remove metadata rows and invalid 2015-12-01 record.
Use monthly variables for model features.
Keep annual variables only for supplementary visual context.
Create china_share, period, and calendar fields (year, month, quarter).
Use capped stay-length and z-score fields for stable clustering.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
5. UI Design
UI design principles in this prototype:

Progressive disclosure: show defaults first, advanced controls optional.
Interpretability-first: output labels use domain terms, not only model terms.
Comparability: consistent color mapping between plot and table.

Detailed storyboard and component mapping are provided at UI Storyboard.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
6. Visual Design & Interactivity Practices
This prototype applies:

Clear visual hierarchy for decision steps.
Explicit mapping between controls and outputs.
Responsive layout for laptop and mobile viewport.
Data-informed defaults to reduce trial-and-error.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
7. Current Status and Next Step
Completed:

Modular app skeleton.
Data integration and smoke-test artifacts.
Quarto write-up scaffold aligned to assignment requirements.

Next:

Add confirmatory stats module.
Add decision-tree module with feature importance panel.
Add deployment pipeline for GitHub Pages + hosted Shiny endpoint.

title: UI Storyboard
text:
Usability Notes

Keep the module order aligned with the final website: visual analysis, clustering, forecasting.
Keep the labels domain-driven and consistent across Proposal, storyboard, and app.
Use the same country names, date windows, and color semantics in all modules.
Make the interaction flow simple enough that users can move from exploration to comparison to forecasting without losing context.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
Prototype: Time Series Clustering
Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
2. Load Packages
The first step is to load the packages used throughout the prototype.

packages <- c("readxl", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "cluster", "knitr")
invisible(lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

package_table <- data.frame(
  Package = packages,
  Version = vapply(packages, function(pkg) as.character(packageVersion(pkg)), character(1))
)

knitr::kable(package_table, align = c("l", "l"))





Package
Version

The package table confirms that the prototype uses a small, base-project-friendly set of tools for reading data, shaping trajectories, clustering country series, and reporting results.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
4. Confirm The Clustering Unit
Before clustering, it is useful to inspect the country series that will be included in the model.

country_summary <- country_long |>
  dplyr::group_by(country) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    first_month = format(min(date), "%Y-%m"),
    last_month = format(max(date), "%Y-%m"),
    missing_arrivals = sum(is.na(arrivals)),
    mean_arrivals = round(mean(arrivals, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(mean_arrivals))

knitr::kable(country_summary, align = c("l", "l", "l", "r", "r"))

This table shows that each row is a country series with a common monthly span. That is exactly the right unit for time series clustering because the model is now grouping recovery trajectories instead of individual months.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
5. Build Comparable Trajectories
The next step is to put every country onto a common scale. Indexing starts each series at 100, and z-scoring then makes the trajectories comparable for distance-based clustering.

country_indexed <- country_wide
for (col in series_cols) {
  first_value <- country_wide[[col]][which(!is.na(country_wide[[col]]))[1]]
  country_indexed[[col]] <- country_wide[[col]] / first_value * 100
}

trajectory_summary <- data.frame(
  Country = series_cols,
  Start_value = round(sapply(series_cols, function(col) country_wide[[col]][1]), 1),
  End_value = round(sapply(series_cols, function(col) country_wide[[col]][nrow(country_wide)]), 1),
  Recovery_ratio = round(
    sapply(
      series_cols,
      function(col) country_wide[[col]][nrow(country_wide)] / country_wide[[col]][1]
    ),
    3
  ),
  Indexed_end = round(sapply(series_cols, function(col) country_indexed[[col]][nrow(country_indexed)]), 1)
)

knitr::kable(
  trajectory_summary |>
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(Recovery_ratio)),
  align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")
)

The trajectory summary shows why normalization is necessary. The countries are measured on the same scale, but their growth paths differ enough that raw values alone would overstate the largest markets.

country_scaled <- country_indexed
country_scaled[series_cols] <- scale(country_scaled[series_cols])

series_matrix <- t(as.matrix(country_scaled[series_cols]))
rownames(series_matrix) <- series_cols

The standardized matrix is now ready for a distance-based clustering model where each row is one country trajectory and each column is one month.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
6. Diagnose The Number Of Clusters
The first diagnostic is a summary table of candidate k values from 2 to 6.

cluster_distance <- dist(series_matrix, method = "euclidean")
cluster_tree <- hclust(cluster_distance, method = "ward.D2")

within_cluster_distance <- function(labels, dmat) {
  d <- as.matrix(dmat)
  total <- 0
  for (grp in unique(labels)) {
    idx <- which(labels == grp)
    if (length(idx) > 1) {
      total <- total + sum(d[idx, idx][upper.tri(d[idx, idx])])
    }
  }
  total
}

candidate_k <- 2:6
k_diagnostics <- do.call(
  rbind,
  lapply(candidate_k, function(k) {
    labels <- cutree(cluster_tree, k = k)
    sil <- silhouette(labels, cluster_distance)
    data.frame(
      k = k,
      mean_silhouette = round(mean(sil[, "sil_width"], na.rm = TRUE), 3),
      within_cluster_distance = round(within_cluster_distance(labels, cluster_distance), 1),
      min_cluster_size = min(table(labels))
    )
  })
)

knitr::kable(k_diagnostics, align = c("r", "r", "r", "r"))




k
mean_silhouette
within_cluster_distance
min_cluster_size




2
0.199
107.2
5


3
0.287
67.3
2


4
0.228
45.3
1


5
0.197
30.0
1


6
0.146
20.0
1





The diagnostics already point to a compact solution with a small number of clusters. In this dataset, k = 3 gives the best balance between separation and interpretability.

6.1 Diagnostic Plot
The second diagnostic is the silhouette trend across candidate k values.

ggplot(k_diagnostics, aes(x = k, y = mean_silhouette)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0f3d3e", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(color = "#d86f45", size = 2.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = candidate_k) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Silhouette Score by Candidate k",
    x = "Number of clusters (k)",
    y = "Mean silhouette score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)







Figure 1





The plot confirms that k = 3 is the strongest practical choice. It separates the countries cleanly enough to be useful while still leaving room for a meaningful market-state interpretation.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
7. Fit The Final Hierarchical Model
With the diagnostics in hand, the final clustering model can be fitted using three clusters.

selected_k <- 3
final_labels <- cutree(cluster_tree, k = selected_k)

prototype_panel <- list(
  raw_wide = country_wide[, c("date", series_cols)],
  normalized_wide = country_indexed[, c("date", series_cols)],
  matrix = series_matrix,
  dates = country_wide$date,
  series = series_cols,
  normalization = "indexed"
)

cluster_solution <- summarize_cluster_solution(
  panel = prototype_panel,
  cluster_id = final_labels,
  distance_matrix = cluster_distance,
  china_series = china_series
)

knitr::kable(
  cluster_solution$summary |>
    dplyr::select(
      cluster,
      cluster_label,
      n_series,
      representative_series,
      avg_end_index,
      avg_trough_index,
      avg_rebound_multiple
    ),
  align = c("l", "l", "r", "l", "r", "r", "r")
)













cluster
cluster_label
n_series
representative_series
avg_end_index
avg_trough_index
avg_rebound_multiple




Cluster 1
Delayed recovery

The final solution produces three interpretable blocks, but the richer point is that they are not just different groups of countries. They also reflect different recovery strengths. The summary table now shows which cluster rebounds furthest, which one experienced the deepest trough, and which country acts as the representative pattern for each group.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
8. Show Cluster Membership
The next table lists every country and its final cluster assignment.

membership_table <- cluster_solution$membership |>
  dplyr::select(
    series,
    cluster,
    cluster_label,
    silhouette,
    end_index,
    trough_index,
    rebound_multiple
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(cluster, dplyr::desc(end_index), series)

This membership view is the direct model output that the Shiny app should expose. It now does more than list cluster IDs: it also shows whether each country finishes the window strongly, how deep its trough becomes, and how much it rebounds from that trough.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
9. Show Representative Cluster Profiles
To make the clusters easier to interpret, the next plot shows the average indexed trajectory of each cluster over time, with China highlighted on top of its cluster profile.

china_line <- data.frame(
  date = country_indexed$date,
  indexed_value = country_indexed[[china_series]],
  series = china_series
)

ggplot(cluster_solution$plot_data, aes(x = date, y = value, color = cluster, group = interaction(series, type))) +
  geom_line(
    data = subset(cluster_solution$plot_data, type == "Series"),
    alpha = 0.14,
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = subset(cluster_solution$plot_data, type == "Cluster mean"),
    linewidth = 1.15
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = china_line,
    aes(x = date, y = indexed_value),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 1.2,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Cluster 1" = "#0f6b5b", "Cluster 2" = "#ef9f23", "Cluster 3" = "#4c6faf")) +
  labs(
    title = "Representative Cluster Trajectories with China Highlighted",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Indexed arrivals (first month = 100)",
    color = "Cluster"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~cluster, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








This plot is now closer to what the final app should show: all member trajectories sit faintly in the background, the cluster mean gives the dominant pattern, and China is highlighted as a dashed reference line. That makes it much easier to tell whether China is representative of its group or slightly offset from it.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
10. Compare Recovery Strength Across Countries
The next chart turns the clustering result into a recovery comparison. Countries that fell lower on the x-axis experienced a deeper trough, while countries that sit higher on the y-axis end the window from a stronger position.

ggplot(
  cluster_solution$series_features,
  aes(x = trough_index, y = end_index, color = cluster, label = series)
) +
  geom_point(size = 3.2, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(vjust = -0.8, size = 3.3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Recovery Position Map",
    subtitle = "Lower trough values indicate deeper shocks, while higher final values indicate stronger rebound",
    x = "Lowest indexed level in the window",
    y = "Final indexed level in the window",
    color = "Cluster"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)








This view adds analytical depth because it separates two ideas that line charts often blur together: how hard each market was hit, and how strongly it recovered by the end of the selected window. The clusters are not just visually different; they also occupy different recovery positions.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
11. Interpret China’s Placement
China belongs to the North Asia cluster, together with Japan and Hong Kong SAR (China). The next table shows that cluster and orders the members by their distance to China in the standardized trajectory space.

knitr::kable(
  cluster_solution$china_context |>
    dplyr::select(series, cluster, cluster_label, distance_to_china, end_index, trough_index),
  align = c("l", "l", "l", "r", "r", "r")
)












series
cluster
cluster_label
distance_to_china
end_index
trough_index




china
Cluster 1
Delayed recovery
0.000
54.5
0


japan
Cluster 1
Delayed recovery
4.994
79.8
0


hong_kong_sar_china
Cluster 1
Delayed recovery
5.014
62.0
0





China falls in Cluster 1 (Delayed recovery). Its closest peers in the selected set are japan, hong_kong_sar_china, and the representative series for this cluster is japan.
This is the most important interpretive message in the clustering module. China remains central to the story, but the model does not treat it as a standalone exception. Instead, it places China inside a specific recovery family, which helps the final application compare it to the right peers rather than to every market at once.

title: Prototype: Time Series Clustering
text:
12. UI Design Implications
The clustering prototype should expose controls that let the user change the country set, the normalization choice, and the number of clusters without breaking the workflow.

ui_map <- data.frame(
  Control = c(
    "Country series selector",
    "Year window",
    "Normalization mode",
    "Cluster count k",
    "Run button",
    "Insight cards",
    "Recovery position map"
  ),
  Purpose = c(
    "Choose the series that will be treated as trajectories",
    "Keep the analysis window aligned with the monthly data",
    "Switch between raw, indexed, and z-scored trajectories",
    "Test whether the grouping should be more or less granular",
    "Trigger clustering only when the user is ready",
    "Explain cluster quality and China's placement in plain language",
    "Compare shock depth and end-of-window recovery strength"
  ),
  Output = c(
    "Membership table",
    "Comparable time-series matrix",
    "Diagnostic table and profile plot",
    "Cluster summary and China placement",
    "Updated cluster assignments and representative profiles",
    "Cluster narrative and China narrative",
    "Recovery metrics table and position plot"
  )
)

knitr::kable(ui_map, align = c("l", "l", "l"))









Control
Purpose
Output




Country series selector
Choose the series that will be treated as trajectories
Membership table


Year window
Keep the analysis window aligned with the monthly data
Comparable time-series matrix


Normalization mode
Switch between raw, indexed, and z-scored trajectories
Diagnostic table and profile plot


Cluster count k
Test whether the grouping should be more or less granular
Cluster summary and China placement


Run button
Trigger clustering only when the user is ready
Updated cluster assignments and representative profiles


Insight cards
Explain cluster quality and China’s placement in plain language
Cluster narrative and China narrative


Recovery position map
Compare shock depth and end-of-window recovery strength
Recovery metrics table and position plot





This UI mapping is the bridge from the prototype page to the final Shiny module. The refinement here is that the app should not stop at showing a cluster ID. It should also explain what the cluster means, how China fits into it, and how recovery strength differs across the grouped markets.

title: Project Home
text:
Visual Analytics · Quarto + Shiny · AY2025/26





Did the recovery of Chinese visitor demand help Singapore shift from pandemic shock to high-performance tourism states?
This site is an integrated workspace for:

Take-home Exercise 2 write-up.
Prototype module design and validation.
Team collaboration and onboarding.
Linked Shiny module development.

title: Project Home
text:
Why This Structure
The information architecture is adapted from the strengths of these references:

Habari Tanzania: one-page storytelling with section anchors and team visibility.
Decoding Chaos: Quarto-native multi-page prototype navigation.

title: Project Home
text:
Workstreams


A. Submission-Ready Report
take-home-ex2.qmd is the individual submission page in Quarto HTML format.


B. Prototype Module
prototype/module-cluster.qmd defines module logic, parameters, UI, and output behavior.


C. Running Shiny App
app/app.R with modular server code in app/R/.


D. Shared Team Base

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
Singapore tourism recovery is best understood as a time-series comparison problem rather than a single-market recovery story. The new full dataset gives the project monthly visitor arrivals by country and by transport mode, which makes it possible to study how different markets recovered at different speeds and with different seasonal patterns.
This proposal builds on the updated project direction recommended by the instructor. Instead of centering the whole application on month-based clustering or tree-based occupancy classification, the final system will focus on three linked analytical modules: time series visual analysis, time series clustering, and time series forecasting.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
Singapore tourism recovery is best understood as a time-series comparison problem rather than a single-market recovery story. The new full dataset gives the project monthly visitor arrivals by country and by transport mode, which makes it possible to study how different markets recovered at different speeds and with different seasonal patterns.
This proposal builds on the updated project direction recommended by the instructor. Instead of centering the whole application on month-based clustering or tree-based occupancy classification, the final system will focus on three linked analytical modules: time series visual analysis, time series clustering, and time series forecasting.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
2. Problem Statement
Tourism recovery discussions often stop at a single headline number such as total arrivals. That is not enough for a visual analytics project. A more useful question is how the recovery path differs across markets, transport modes, and time windows, and how those differences can be communicated in an interactive Shiny application.
The project therefore needs a common time-series workflow that lets users:

compare multiple countries or transport series over time,
identify groups of series with similar recovery trajectories, and
forecast future arrivals for selected series.

Without this structure, the analysis remains fragmented and the application cannot support a coherent user journey.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
3. Data and Scope
The project now uses the instructor-provided full tourism workbook, which contains monthly arrival series for Singapore and a large set of countries and transport modes. The main analytical window is the continuous monthly block from December 2016 to February 2026.

Main data characteristics

Monthly frequency for the core arrival series
Country-level arrival counts
Transport-mode splits such as Air, Sea, and Land
Strong seasonality across major markets
Very low missingness in the monthly arrival block



Core series for the final app

Visitor Arrivals
Visitor Arrivals: China
Visitor Arrivals: India
Visitor Arrivals: Malaysia
Visitor Arrivals: Australia
Visitor Arrivals: Japan
Visitor Arrivals: Philippines
Visitor Arrivals: United Kingdom
Visitor Arrivals: Germany
Visitor Arrivals: Air
Visitor Arrivals: Sea
Visitor Arrivals: Land

The project will treat these series as the common analytical base for all three modules.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
4. Project Aim
The project aims to develop a visual analytics prototype that helps users understand Singapore tourism recovery through comparison, clustering, and forecasting.
The application should allow users to:

compare the trajectories of selected countries and transport modes,
cluster series with similar time-series behavior,
forecast future arrivals for selected series, and
move smoothly between exploration, explanation, and prediction.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
5. Research Questions
The proposal is guided by four questions:

How did selected source markets differ in recovery timing and seasonal behavior?
Which country or transport series have similar monthly arrival trajectories?
How strongly does the Chinese market behave relative to other key markets in the recovery period?
Can the project forecast future arrivals with enough structure to support a useful Shiny interface?

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
6. Analytical Modules
The final application is organized into three modules.

6.1 Time Series Visual Analysis
This module is the entry point for the application. It will show monthly arrival trajectories for selected countries and transport modes, with options to switch between raw values, indexed values, and share-based comparisons.
The main purpose of this module is to answer questions such as:

Which countries recovered fastest?
How did China’s relative contribution change over time?
Did Air, Sea, and Land recover in the same way?

The final UI should expose controls for series selection, time window selection, and display mode switching. The output should combine a clear line chart, an indexed comparison chart, and a concise summary table.


6.2 Time Series Clustering
The clustering module is designed to compare series, not months. This is the key pivot in the project. The cluster unit will be a country series or transport series, and the goal will be to group series with similar monthly arrival patterns.
This is a better fit for the data and for the instructor feedback because it turns clustering into a trajectory comparison tool. For example, China may cluster with other markets that show similar disruption and recovery timing, while markets such as Malaysia or Australia may form a different recovery group.
The module will use:

a selected set of countries or transport series,
a normalization step such as indexed or z-score transformation,
a similarity measure suitable for time-series comparison, and
a clustering method that produces interpretable groups.

The output should include:

a cluster membership table,
a representative pattern plot,
a cluster diagnostic graphic,
a recovery position map,
a short cluster insight block that explains the strongest rebound group and China’s placement, and
a downloadable assignment table.



6.3 Time Series Forecasting
The forecasting module will complete the analytical workflow by estimating future values for selected arrival series. This gives the app a forward-looking component and makes the final Shiny system more than a descriptive dashboard.
The forecasting task will likely compare a simple baseline with at least one time-series model, then display forecast accuracy and predicted future trajectories.
This module should support:

a target series selector,
a training cutoff control,
a forecast horizon setting,
a model choice control, and
forecast download output.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
7. UI Design and Storyboard
The UI design follows a three-module structure:

Time Series Visual Analysis
Time Series Clustering
Time Series Forecasting

The storyboard will document the user journey from exploration to comparison to prediction. It will show which controls are exposed in each module, what output is generated, and how the user can move from one analytical question to the next.
The design principle is to keep each module focused:

the visual analysis module explains what changed,
the clustering module explains which series behave similarly, and
the forecasting module explains what is likely to happen next.

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
8. R Packages
The proposal relies on packages that support time-series data import, transformation, analysis, clustering, forecasting, and the Shiny interface.



Package
Purpose




readxl
Import the workbook


dplyr
Transform and filter monthly series


tidyr
Reshape data between long and wide formats


lubridate
Work with monthly dates


ggplot2
Build the core charts


plotly
Add interactivity where useful


DT
Display interactive tables


patchwork
Combine multiple charts


tsibble
Support tidy time-series structures


feasts
Time-series features and diagnostics


fable
Forecasting models


fabletools
Forecasting workflow support


forecast
Baseline forecasting methods


cluster
Clustering utilities


dtwclust
Time-series clustering support


shiny
Interactive application framework


bslib
Layout and theme support


htmlwidgets
Render interactive outputs

title: Singapore Tourism Time-Series Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
9. Expected Contribution
The final system will contribute a coherent time-series visual analytics workflow for Singapore tourism.
It will:

compare key source markets and transport modes over time,
group similar series into interpretable clusters, and
provide a forecasting view that extends the analysis into the future.

Compared with the old project framing, this version is more consistent with the instructor guidance and more suitable for a polished final Shiny application.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
The app follows a modular structure:

app/app.R - top-level layout and module wiring.
app/R/data_utils.R - dataset loading and preprocessing helpers.
app/R/mod_cluster_ui.R - cluster module UI.
app/R/mod_cluster_server.R - cluster module logic.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text: This review covers the clustering prototype draft in prototype/module-cluster.qmd, the supporting cleaned dataset, and the renderability of the updated Quarto page inside the full project.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text: This review covers the clustering prototype draft in prototype/module-cluster.qmd, the supporting cleaned dataset, and the renderability of the updated Quarto page inside the full project.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Findings In The Original Draft

Finding 1: The page still grouped too much code into single blocks
The earlier revision improved reproducibility, but several sections still bundled too many steps together. That made the page harder to read in presentation mode and less faithful to the “one code block, one result, one explanation” rhythm requested for this prototype.


Finding 2: Package loading and data import were not shown explicitly in the first draft
The earlier draft loaded and transformed data in a hidden setup chunk. That made the page less aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference style, where the reader can see the workflow directly.


Finding 3: The stay-duration feature was underspecified
The draft proposed avg_stay_monthly directly for clustering, but the cleaned data preparation already created avg_stay_monthly_capped for modeling. Using the uncapped series would let a small number of pandemic months dominate Euclidean distances.


Finding 4: The choice of k = 3 needed evidence
The draft said “start with K = 3”, but it did not show the elbow pattern, silhouette diagnostics, or the interpretability tradeoff behind the final selection.


Finding 5: The page was not yet submission-ready
The original file was a short module note, not a full prototype report. It lacked a visible end-to-end analytical workflow, detailed results sections, and a stronger explanation layer after each output.


Finding 6: Some narrative statements were too static
After the restructuring, a few explanatory sentences still assumed fixed outcomes, such as which state was the largest. That could drift out of sync if the dataset changed later.


Finding 7: The centroid table labels could be misread
The cluster-center table displayed standardized values, but the column names did not make that explicit enough. A reader could incorrectly read them as raw monthly averages.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Fixes Implemented

Rewrote prototype/module-cluster.qmd again into smaller analytical blocks.
Changed the structure so one focused code chunk now produces one immediate figure or table.
Added a short explanation directly after each output instead of collecting explanations too late.
Kept package loading and data import visible.
Retained avg_stay_monthly_capped as the modeling stay variable.
Preserved the code-generated elbow, silhouette, cluster profile, and timeline outputs.
Replaced static statements with result-driven narrative where appropriate.
Renamed the centroid columns so they clearly indicate standardized values.
Kept the narrative aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference structure while using k-means appropriately for continuous monthly data.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Verification Checklist








Check item
Result
Notes




Small code-output-explanation rhythm implemented
PASS
the page now uses many smaller analytical chunks


Visible package import section included
PASS
packages are loaded in a visible code block


Visible data import section included
PASS

Modeling variables match cleaned data design
PASS
uses visitor_arrivals, china_share, hotel_occ, avg_stay_monthly_capped


Results are generated from code
PASS

Narrative statements are data-driven
PASS
the largest-state and period-distribution text now reads from computed objects


Standardized centers are clearly labeled
PASS
center-table headers now state that values are standardized


UI design section included
PASS
parameter and output exposure are documented via code-generated tables


Quarto page renders successfully
PASS
verified with quarto render prototype/module-cluster.qmd


Review report page renders successfully
PASS
verified with quarto render prototype/wang-zhuoran-review-report.qmd

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Residual Risks

The selected k = 3 solution is substantively stronger than the k = 2 solution, but the silhouette score is slightly lower. This tradeoff should still be acknowledged in the final coursework report.
china_share can look high during collapse months because it is a relative measure. The final narrative should always pair it with absolute arrivals.
If additional monthly indicators are added later, the cluster labels may need to be recalibrated.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Review Outcome
No blocking issues remain in the updated clustering prototype. The page now matches the requested reproducible style much more closely: visible workflow, code-generated results, and explanation after each analytical step.

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.3Read the original data


Show the code
col_names <- c(
  "date",
  "avg_stay_monthly",
  "hotel_occ",
  "spend_per_capita",
  "tourism_receipts",
  "avg_stay_annual",
  "visitor_arrivals",
  "visitor_arrivals_china"
)

raw_tbl <- readxl::read_excel(
  path = raw_path,
  sheet = "My Series",
  skip = 29,
  col_names = FALSE
) %>%
  setNames(col_names) %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    across(-date, as.numeric)
  ) %>%
  arrange(date)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis
text:
1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis


Show the code
monthly_clean <- raw_tbl %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(date),
    !is.na(avg_stay_monthly),
    !is.na(hotel_occ),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals_china)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date),
    quarter = lubridate::quarter(date),
    period = case_when(
      date <= as.Date("2020-01-01") ~ "pre_covid",
      date <= as.Date("2021-12-01") ~ "covid_shock",
      TRUE ~ "recovery"
    ),
    china_share = visitor_arrivals_china / visitor_arrivals,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.6Handling extreme values


Show the code
stay_cap <- quantile(monthly_clean$avg_stay_monthly, probs = 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

monthly_clean <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    avg_stay_monthly_capped = pmin(avg_stay_monthly, stay_cap),
    visitor_arrivals_millions = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    visitor_arrivals_china_thousands = visitor_arrivals_china / 1000
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.7Generate analysis fields


Show the code
occ_cuts <- quantile(monthly_clean$hotel_occ, probs = c(1/3, 2/3), na.rm = TRUE)

analysis_ready <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    cluster_z_visitor_arrivals = as.numeric(scale(visitor_arrivals)),
    cluster_z_china_share = as.numeric(scale(china_share)),
    cluster_z_hotel_occ = as.numeric(scale(hotel_occ)),
    cluster_z_avg_stay_monthly_capped = as.numeric(scale(avg_stay_monthly_capped)),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = case_when(
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[1]] ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[2]] ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    ),
    hotel_occ_level_business = case_when(
      hotel_occ < 70 ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= 85 ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(hotel_occ_level_tertile, levels = c("low", "medium", "high")),
    hotel_occ_level_business = factor(hotel_occ_level_business, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    dataset_split = if_else(row_number() <= floor(n() * 0.8), "train", "test")
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 1.8Generate data specifically for classification tree construction
text:
1.8Generate data specifically for classification tree construction


Show the code
decision_tree_ready <- analysis_ready %>%
  select(
    date, year, month, quarter, period,
    visitor_arrivals, visitor_arrivals_china,
    china_share, china_share_pct,
    hotel_occ, avg_stay_monthly, avg_stay_monthly_capped,
    hotel_occ_level_tertile, hotel_occ_level_business,
    dataset_split
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.9Export the cleaned data


Show the code
readr::write_csv(monthly_clean, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_monthly_clean.csv"))
readr::write_csv(decision_tree_ready, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_decision_tree_ready.csv"))
readr::write_csv(analysis_ready, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_four_part_analysis_ready.csv"))

writexl::write_xlsx(
  list(
    monthly_clean = monthly_clean,
    decision_tree_ready = decision_tree_ready,
    analysis_ready_all = analysis_ready
  ),
  path = file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_four_part_analysis_ready.xlsx")
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.2Reading data


Show the code
df <- readr::read_csv(data_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    month = factor(
      month,
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = c("Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
                 "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec")
    ),
    quarter = factor(quarter),
    period = factor(period, levels = c("pre_covid", "covid_shock", "recovery")),
    dataset_split = factor(dataset_split, levels = c("train", "test")),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(
      hotel_occ_level_tertile,
      levels = c("low", "medium", "high")
    ),

    # 为了让树图上的阈值更易读，创建展示型变量
    arrivals_million = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100,
    stay_days = avg_stay_monthly_capped
  )

section: 2.3Split the dataset into training and testing sets

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.4Build classification tree
Target variable: Hotel occupancy rate level (low / medium / high)
Independent variables: # arrivals_million = Total inbound tourists (in millions)
china_share_pct = Proportion of Chinese tourists (percentage)
stay_days = Truncated average length of stay
month = Month (seasonality)


Show the code
fit_tree <- rpart::rpart(
  hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + china_share_pct + stay_days + month,
  data = train_df,
  method = "class",
  parms = list(split = "gini"),
  control = rpart::rpart.control(
    cp = 0.005,
    maxdepth = 4,
    minsplit = 10,
    minbucket = 5,
    xval = 10
  )
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.5Check the complexity parameter table.


Show the code
printcp(fit_tree)



Classification tree:
rpart::rpart(formula = hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + 
    china_share_pct + stay_days + month, data = train_df, method = "class", 
    parms = list(split = "gini"), control = rpart::rpart.control(cp = 0.005, 
        maxdepth = 4, minsplit = 10, minbucket = 5, xval = 10))

Variables actually used in tree construction:
[1] arrivals_million month           

Root node error: 57/88 = 0.64773

n= 88 

       CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
1 0.49123      0   1.00000 1.21053 0.067715
2 0.21053      1   0.50877 0.54386 0.078614
3 0.04386      2   0.29825 0.36842 0.070151
4 0.00500      4   0.21053 0.38596 0.071263


Show the code
plotcp(fit_tree)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.6Prune the tree


Show the code
bestcp <- fit_tree$cptable[which.min(fit_tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
pruned_tree <- prune(fit_tree, cp = bestcp)

cat("Best cp:", bestcp, "\n")


Best cp: 0.04385965 


Show the code
print(pruned_tree)


n= 88 

node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
      * denotes terminal node

1) root 88 57 low (0.35227273 0.29545455 0.35227273)  
  2) arrivals_million< 0.7575905 31  3 low (0.90322581 0.09677419 0.00000000) *
  3) arrivals_million>=0.7575905 57 26 high (0.05263158 0.40350877 0.54385965)  
    6) month=Jan,May,Dec 16  3 medium (0.12500000 0.81250000 0.06250000) *
    7) month=Feb,Mar,Apr,Jun,Jul,Aug,Sep,Oct,Nov 41 11 high (0.02439024 0.24390244 0.73170732) *

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.7Interactive tree visualization


Show the code
tree_vis <- visNetwork::visTree(
  pruned_tree,
  data = as.data.frame(train_df),
  main = "Decision Tree for Hotel Occupancy Level",
  submain = "Target: low / medium / high",
  width = "100%",
  height = "700px",
  nodesFontSize = 16,
  edgesFontSize = 14,
  fallenLeaves = TRUE,
  legend = TRUE,
  nodesPopSize = TRUE,
  minNodeSize = 14,
  maxNodeSize = 32,
  highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, hover = TRUE),
  export = TRUE
)

tree_vis








Show the code
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(
  tree_vis,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_visual.html"),
  selfcontained = FALSE
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.8Static tree visualization for report


Show the code
png(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_static.png"),
  width = 1800,
  height = 1200,
  res = 180
)

rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
  pruned_tree,
  type = 2,
  extra = 104,
  under = TRUE,
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  faclen = 0,
  tweak = 1.15,
  main = "Decision Tree for Hotel Occupancy Level"
)

dev.off()


png 
  2

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.9Predict on test set


Show the code
pred_class <- predict(pruned_tree, newdata = test_df, type = "class")
pred_prob  <- predict(pruned_tree, newdata = test_df, type = "prob") %>%
  as.data.frame()

# 统一 levels，避免 confusionMatrix 报错
pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile <- factor(
  test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile,
  levels = c("low", "medium", "high")
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.10Evaluate model performance


Show the code
cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(
  data = pred_class,
  reference = test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile
)

print(cm)


Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction low medium high
    low      0      0    0
    medium   2      4    0
    high     4      6    6

Overall Statistics
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.4545          
                 95% CI : (0.2439, 0.6779)
    No Information Rate : 0.4545          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.581967        
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.1951          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.007383        

Statistics by Class:

                     Class: low Class: medium Class: high
Sensitivity              0.0000        0.4000      1.0000
Specificity              1.0000        0.8333      0.3750
Pos Pred Value              NaN        0.6667      0.3750
Neg Pred Value           0.7273        0.6250      1.0000
Prevalence               0.2727        0.4545      0.2727
Detection Rate           0.0000        0.1818      0.2727
Detection Prevalence     0.0000        0.2727      0.7273
Balanced Accuracy        0.5000        0.6167      0.6875


Show the code
metrics_tbl <- tibble(
  metric = c("accuracy", "kappa"),
  value = c(cm$overall[["Accuracy"]], cm$overall[["Kappa"]])
)

print(metrics_tbl)


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  metric   value
  <chr>    <dbl>
1 accuracy 0.455
2 kappa    0.195

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.11Variable importance


Show the code
importance_tbl <- tibble(
  variable = names(pruned_tree$variable.importance),
  importance = as.numeric(pruned_tree$variable.importance)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(importance))

print(importance_tbl)


# A tibble: 4 × 2
  variable         importance
  <chr>                 <dbl>
1 arrivals_million      22.4 
2 stay_days             21.6 
3 china_share_pct       10.1 
4 month                  8.99

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.12Export results


Show the code
write.csv(
  as.data.frame(cm$table),
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_confusion_matrix.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  metrics_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_metrics.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  importance_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_variable_importance.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

prediction_tbl <- bind_cols(
  test_df %>% select(date, hotel_occ, hotel_occ_level_tertile),
  tibble(predicted_class = pred_class),
  pred_prob
)

write.csv(
  prediction_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_test_predictions.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

cat("Decision tree analysis complete.\n")


Decision tree analysis complete.


Show the code
cat("Outputs saved to:", output_dir, "\n")

pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile <- factor(
  test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile,
  levels = c("low", "medium", "high")
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.4Confusion matrix heatmap


Show the code
conf_tbl <- as.data.frame(cm$table)

conf_plot <- ggplot(conf_tbl, aes(x = Reference, y = Prediction, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f7fbff", high = "#08519c") +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Confusion Matrix",
    x = "Actual class",
    y = "Predicted class",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(conf_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_confusion_heatmap.png"),
  plot = conf_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.5Variable importance plot


Show the code
importance_plot <- importance_tbl %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Overall, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7fb8") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Variable Importance",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(importance_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_importance.png"),
  plot = importance_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.6OOB accuracy vs number of trees


Show the code
tree_grid <- seq(50, 300, by = 25)

oob_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(tree_grid, function(nt) {
  mod <- ranger::ranger(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + china_share_pct + stay_days + month,
    data = train_df,
    num.trees = nt,
    mtry = rf_model$bestTune$mtry,
    min.node.size = rf_model$bestTune$min.node.size,
    splitrule = as.character(rf_model$bestTune$splitrule),
    importance = "impurity",
    seed = 1234
  )

  tibble(
    num_trees = nt,
    oob_error = mod$prediction.error,
    oob_accuracy = 1 - mod$prediction.error
  )
})

print(oob_tbl)


# A tibble: 11 × 3
   num_trees oob_error oob_accuracy
       <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
 1        50     0.318        0.682
 2        75     0.318        0.682
 3       100     0.307        0.693
 4       125     0.295        0.705
 5       150     0.284        0.716
 6       175     0.284        0.716
 7       200     0.295        0.705
 8       225     0.295        0.705
 9       250     0.295        0.705
10       275     0.341        0.659
11       300     0.330        0.670


Show the code
oob_plot <- ggplot(oob_tbl, aes(x = num_trees, y = oob_accuracy)) +
  geom_line(color = "#238b45", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#238b45", size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "OOB Accuracy vs Number of Trees",
    x = "Number of trees",
    y = "OOB accuracy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(oob_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_oob_accuracy.png"),
  plot = oob_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.7Actual vs predicted over time


Show the code
pred_plot_df <- test_df %>%
  transmute(
    date,
    actual = hotel_occ_level_tertile,
    predicted = pred_class
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(actual, predicted),
    names_to = "series",
    values_to = "level"
  )

pred_plot <- ggplot(pred_plot_df, aes(x = date, y = level, color = series, shape = series)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs Predicted Hotel Occupancy Level",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Occupancy level"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(actual = "#d95f0e", predicted = "#3182bd")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(pred_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_actual_vs_predicted.png"),
  plot = pred_plot,
  width = 9,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.8Export results


Show the code
write.csv(
  metrics_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_metrics.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  conf_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_confusion_matrix.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  importance_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_variable_importance.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  oob_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_oob_accuracy.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

prediction_tbl <- bind_cols(
  test_df %>% select(date, hotel_occ_level_tertile),
  tibble(predicted_class = pred_class),
  pred_prob
)

write.csv(
  prediction_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_test_predictions.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

cat("Random forest analysis complete.\n")
